title: HashiCorp Vault Secrets Management Quiz
description: Test how you run HashiCorp Vault in production: dynamic database credentials, leases and TTLs, least-privilege policies, AppRole and Kubernetes auth, the Transit engine, auto-unseal, audit logging, and fast revocation when something gets compromised.

questions.title: Secrets in Plain Sight
questions.description: A teammate says the Kubernetes Secret already protects the database password. What is the real problem here?
questions.situation: Your services read the production database password from a Kubernetes Secret. During a review, someone with read access to the namespace runs the command below and the password prints to their terminal in seconds. The team assumed the value was encrypted because it looked like random characters.
questions.codeExample:
$ kubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
Sup3rS3cret-Prod-2026

$ kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml
data:
  password: U3VwM3JTM2NyZXQtUHJvZC0yMDI2   # base64, not encryption
options: Kubernetes Secrets use AES-256 by default, so the only fix is rotating the password more often
Secrets stored this way are only base64-encoded, with no access control, audit trail, or rotation, which is what Vault adds
The password is fine because base64 makes it unreadable to anyone without the decode command
The issue is the YAML output format, so switching to JSON output keeps the value hidden
questions.explanation: base64 is encoding, not encryption. Anyone who can read the Secret can decode it instantly. Kubernetes only encrypts Secrets at rest if you explicitly turn on an EncryptionConfiguration with a KMS provider, and even then anyone with API read access still sees the plaintext. Vault solves the bigger picture: secrets live in one place behind identity-based access policies, every read is written to an audit log, and you can issue short-lived or dynamic credentials instead of one shared static password. The output format (YAML vs JSON) changes nothing about who can read the value.
questions.hint: Look closely at what base64 actually does to the value, and ask what is missing around storage: who can read it, and is there any record of who did?

questions.title: Every Request Returns 503
questions.description: After a node reboot, every Vault call fails. What do you need to do before secrets work again?
questions.situation: Your Vault server restarted overnight. This morning every application that reads from Vault is throwing errors, and your own CLI calls fail too. You check the server status and see it came back up but is refusing to serve any secrets.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault status
Key                Value
---                -----
Seal Type          shamir
Initialized        true
Sealed             true
Total Shares       5
Threshold          3

$ curl -s https://vault.internal:8200/v1/secret/data/app | jq .errors
["Vault is sealed"]
options: Run vault operator init again to generate a fresh master key and bring it online
Restart the Vault process one more time, since sealing clears itself after the service stabilizes
Provide the unseal key shares up to the threshold (3 of 5) so Vault can reconstruct the master key and unseal
Log in with the root token, which automatically unseals the server on first use
questions.explanation: Vault starts sealed every time the process restarts. While sealed, it knows where the encrypted data is but cannot decrypt it because the master key is not in memory. To unseal, you supply unseal key shares until you reach the threshold (here 3 of 5), and Vault reconstructs the master key in memory. The root token is for authentication after unsealing, not for unsealing. Restarting again does nothing. And running vault operator init on an already-initialized server will not re-init it, but treating init as the fix is exactly the kind of mistake that risks data loss, so never reach for it here. The real long-term fix is auto-unseal so a restart does not page anyone.
questions.hint: Vault comes up sealed on every restart by design. Think about what reconstructs the master key in memory, and notice the Threshold value in the status output.

questions.title: Everything Uses the Root Token
questions.description: Each microservice authenticates to Vault with the initial root token. Why is this a problem and what should you do?
questions.situation: You inherit a Vault setup where every service and every CI job is configured with the same token. You check it and realize it is the original root token created during vault operator init. It has no expiry and can do anything, including delete every secrets engine and create new admin tokens.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault token lookup
Key                 Value
---                 -----
id                  hvs.xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
policies            ["root"]
ttl                 0s          # never expires
renewable           false

# Same token found in: app configs, CI variables, a Slack message
options: Define least-privilege policies, issue scoped tokens through an auth method per workload, then revoke the root token
Keep the root token but rotate its value every 90 days so a leak has a limited window
Wrap the root token in a Kubernetes Secret so only pods can reach it, which makes it safe to reuse
Set a TTL on the root token so it expires and forces services to re-fetch it automatically
questions.explanation: The root token is meant for initial setup and emergency break-glass, not day-to-day app access. The fix is to give each workload its own identity through an auth method (AppRole, Kubernetes, and so on), attach a policy that grants only the paths it needs, and let it receive a short-lived, scoped token. Once real auth is in place, revoke the root token entirely; you can always regenerate a new one later with the unseal key shares if you genuinely need it. Rotating or wrapping the root token still leaves every service over-privileged, and the root token ignores normal TTL handling, so none of those options reduce the blast radius.
questions.hint: The root token is for break-glass and first-time setup, not normal traffic. Think about giving each workload its own identity and only the access it needs.

questions.title: Stop Sharing One DB Password
questions.description: One static Postgres password is shared by every service and rotating it means a coordinated redeploy. Which Vault feature fixes this?
questions.situation: Your platform has a single Postgres password baked into every service. It leaked once in a log, and rotating it required redeploying eight services at the same time to avoid downtime. You want each service to get its own credentials that expire on their own, so a leak is short-lived and rotation is automatic.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault read database/creds/readonly
Key                Value
---                -----
lease_id           database/creds/readonly/3xKp...
lease_duration     1h
username           v-token-readonly-9fA2bQ
password           A1b2-C3d4-E5f6-G7h8

# Vault created this Postgres user on the fly and will drop it at expiry
options: Move the static password into the KV v2 engine so it is versioned and can be rolled back
Use the database secrets engine to generate dynamic, short-lived credentials unique to each request
Encrypt the shared password with the Transit engine so services decrypt it at startup
Store the password with response wrapping so each service unwraps its own copy once
questions.explanation: The database secrets engine connects Vault to your database with an admin role and generates fresh credentials on demand. Each service gets a unique username and password tied to a lease, and Vault drops the database user automatically when the lease ends or is revoked. That kills the shared-password problem: a leak only exposes one short-lived account, and rotation happens continuously instead of as a risky redeploy. KV v2 still stores a static secret, just with version history. Transit encrypts data but does not create database users. Response wrapping is a delivery mechanism for an existing secret, not a way to generate new ones.
questions.hint: Notice that the username in the output was generated by Vault and has a lease. You want credentials that are created per request and expire on their own, not a better way to store one shared value.

questions.title: Connections Drop After an Hour
questions.description: An app fetches dynamic DB credentials at startup and works fine, then loses database access about an hour later. What is happening?
questions.situation: A service reads dynamic Postgres credentials from Vault when it boots, opens a connection pool, and runs fine. Around 60 minutes in, every query starts failing with an authentication error, even though nothing was deployed and the network is healthy. Restarting the pod fixes it for another hour.
questions.codeExample:
# at startup
$ vault read database/creds/app
lease_id        database/creds/app/8aQ...
lease_duration  1h
renewable       true

# ~60 min later, in the app logs
FATAL: password authentication failed for user "v-token-app-7Yk"
options: The connection pool is too small, so increase max connections and the auth errors stop
It is a transient network partition to the database that happens to recur every hour
The credential lease expired and Vault revoked the database user, so the app must renew the lease (or re-fetch) before it ends
The KV TTL elapsed, so set the KV secret to never expire and the credentials stay valid
questions.explanation: Dynamic secrets come with a lease and a TTL. When the lease_duration elapses, Vault revokes the credentials at the backend, which here means dropping the Postgres user, so existing sessions and any reconnect start failing. The app needs to renew the lease before it expires (renewable is true) or fetch a new credential and refresh its pool. The cleanest way is to run Vault Agent, which renews leases and rewrites credentials for you. The restart hides the symptom because the app fetches a fresh lease on boot. Pool size and network theories do not explain a clean one-hour cycle, and this is not a KV secret, so KV TTL settings are irrelevant.
questions.hint: Look at lease_duration in the startup output and compare it to when failures begin. Dynamic credentials do not live forever unless something keeps them alive.

questions.title: One Token Reads Everything
questions.description: An app token can read every secret under secret/, including other teams' data. Which policy gives it only what it needs?
questions.situation: During an audit you find the billing service token can list and read all of secret/. It only needs the secrets under its own path. You are on KV version 2, which is mounted at secret/ and stores values under a data/ prefix in the API path. You need to rewrite the policy for least privilege.
questions.codeExample:
# current policy (too broad)
path "secret/*" {
  capabilities = ["read", "list"]
}

# KV v2 read path looks like:
#   secret/data/billing/stripe
#   secret/data/billing/paypal
options: path "secret/*" { capabilities = ["read", "list", "deny"] }  to block other teams' paths
path "secret/billing/*" { capabilities = ["read", "sudo"] }  to read only billing with elevated rights
path "secret/metadata/billing/*" { capabilities = ["read"] }  using the metadata path to fetch billing values
path "secret/data/billing/*" { capabilities = ["read"] }  to read only the billing secrets
questions.explanation: With KV v2, the actual secret values live under the data/ prefix, so a read policy must target secret/data/billing/*, not secret/billing/*. Scoping to that path with only the read capability gives least privilege. Mixing deny into a list of capabilities does not work the way people expect; deny is its own rule that overrides others and you do not combine it with read like that. Adding sudo grants root-level access to special endpoints and is the opposite of least privilege. The metadata/ path only exposes version info and metadata, not the secret values themselves, so that policy would not let the app read its secrets at all.
questions.hint: KV v2 hides the values one level deeper than the mount path. Check what prefix the read path actually uses, and grant the smallest capability that does the job.

questions.title: How Does the App Log In?
questions.description: A CI job and a service need to authenticate to Vault without a hardcoded long-lived token. What is the right approach?
questions.situation: You are removing the static token your CI pipeline uses to talk to Vault. The pipeline runs outside Kubernetes, so you cannot use the Kubernetes auth method. You want the job to log in, get a short-lived token scoped by policy, and you want to avoid leaving a usable secret sitting in the repo or CI config.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault write auth/approle/login \
    role_id=$ROLE_ID \
    secret_id=$SECRET_ID
Key             Value
---             -----
token           hvs.CAES...    # short-lived, scoped by policy
token_policies  ["ci-deploy"]
token_ttl       20m
options: Use AppRole: a non-secret role_id plus a short-lived secret_id (delivered wrapped) returns a scoped token
Generate one long-lived token, store it as a masked CI variable, and reuse it for every run
Authenticate with the root token in CI, since pipelines are trusted infrastructure anyway
Put the role_id and secret_id together in the repo so any runner can log in without setup
questions.explanation: AppRole is built for machine and automation logins that cannot use a platform-native method. The role_id is like a username and is not secret on its own. The secret_id is the sensitive half; you generate it short-lived and ideally deliver it response-wrapped so it is single-use and tamper-evident. The job exchanges both for a token scoped by the role's policies with a short TTL. Reusing one long-lived token just recreates the static-secret problem. Using the root token in CI is a huge blast radius. And committing the secret_id to the repo defeats the whole point, since anyone who reads the repo can authenticate.
questions.hint: Think about splitting the credential into a non-secret identifier and a sensitive, short-lived half. Which method is designed for automation that cannot use Kubernetes or cloud identity?

questions.title: Pods Need Secrets Without Static Tokens
questions.description: Workloads in Kubernetes need to read from Vault, but you do not want to distribute tokens to pods. How do they authenticate?
questions.situation: You are wiring up Vault for apps running in Kubernetes. You do not want to bake a Vault token into images or push secret_ids into ConfigMaps. Each deployment already runs under its own ServiceAccount, and you want Vault to trust that identity and hand back a scoped token.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault write auth/kubernetes/role/billing \
    bound_service_account_names=billing \
    bound_service_account_namespaces=payments \
    policies=billing-read \
    ttl=1h

# pod presents its projected ServiceAccount JWT to log in
options: Mount the root token into each pod as a Secret and let the app use it directly
Use the Kubernetes auth method: the pod presents its ServiceAccount JWT, Vault verifies it via the TokenReview API and returns a scoped token
Store an AppRole secret_id in a ConfigMap so every pod in the namespace can log in
Give every pod the same shared token and rely on network policies to limit who reaches Vault
questions.explanation: The Kubernetes auth method lets a pod authenticate with the ServiceAccount token Kubernetes already projects into it. Vault calls the cluster's TokenReview API to confirm the token is valid and identifies the ServiceAccount, then issues a Vault token scoped by the matching role's policies. No long-lived Vault credential ships in the image. Mounting the root token is a severe over-grant. Putting a secret_id in a ConfigMap exposes it to anyone who can read ConfigMaps, since they are not secret. A single shared token plus network policy still means one leaked token unlocks everything; identity per workload is the point.
questions.hint: Kubernetes already gives every pod a verifiable identity through its ServiceAccount. Which Vault method validates that identity with the cluster instead of shipping a secret into the pod?

questions.title: Encrypt PII Without Holding the Key
questions.description: Your app must encrypt user data before storing it, but the team does not want to manage or store the encryption key. Which engine fits?
questions.situation: A new feature stores sensitive user fields that must be encrypted at the application layer. The security team does not want the AES key living in your codebase, your config, or your database. They want a central place to hold the key, rotate it, and do encrypt and decrypt operations, while your app only ever handles ciphertext.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault write transit/encrypt/pii \
    plaintext=$(echo -n '123-45-6789' | base64)
Key            Value
---            -----
ciphertext     vault:v1:8SDd3W...

# app stores 'vault:v1:...' and calls transit/decrypt to read it back
options: Store a generated AES key in KV v2 and have each service fetch it to encrypt locally
Use the PKI engine to issue a certificate and encrypt the data with its public key
Use the Transit engine: Vault holds the key and performs encrypt and decrypt, so the app never sees the key
Hash the data with the Transit engine so it cannot be reversed and store the hash
questions.explanation: Transit is encryption as a service. The key never leaves Vault; your app sends plaintext, gets back ciphertext tagged with a key version (vault:v1:...), and calls decrypt to reverse it. Because the key stays in Vault you get central key rotation and the ability to rewrap old ciphertext to a new key version without re-encrypting in your app. Storing an AES key in KV defeats the goal, since the key now lives where your services and anyone reading KV can grab it. PKI is for issuing and signing certificates, not bulk data encryption. Transit can also do HMAC, but hashing is one-way and you need to decrypt this data later.
questions.hint: The requirement is that the key never lives in your app or database. Which engine does the encrypt and decrypt for you and keeps the key inside Vault?

questions.title: Someone Overwrote the Secret
questions.description: A bad deploy overwrote the production API key in KV with an empty value. Can you get the old one back?
questions.situation: A pipeline bug wrote a blank value over the live payment API key stored in KV. Services are failing. You are on the KV version 2 engine. You need the previous value back fast, and you want to stop this from silently happening again.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault kv metadata get secret/payments/api-key
====== Versions ======
Version    Created Time           Deletion Time
1          2026-05-01T10:00:00Z   n/a
2          2026-06-16T09:14:00Z   n/a   <- current, blank value

$ vault kv get -version=1 secret/payments/api-key   # ?
options: The old value is gone; KV only keeps the latest write, so restore the whole cluster from a Raft snapshot
Run vault kv undelete, which rolls every secret in the mount back to its previous state at once
Only a root token can read old versions, so escalate and read the prior value with root
KV v2 keeps version history, so read version 1 to recover the value and consider check-and-set to prevent blind overwrites
questions.explanation: KV version 2 keeps a history of writes, so the previous value is still there. You read it with vault kv get -version=1 and write it back, or use the metadata to roll forward to a known-good value. To stop blind overwrites, turn on check-and-set (cas_required) so a write must reference the version it expects, which makes a racing or buggy write fail instead of clobbering. A full cluster snapshot restore is a heavy, disruptive answer when the data is one command away. undelete works on a deleted version, not as a mount-wide rollback. And version history is governed by policy capabilities on the metadata and data paths, not by needing the root token.
questions.hint: You are on KV v2. The metadata output is telling you something important about what Vault kept. Also think about how to make the next overwrite fail loudly.

questions.title: Who Read This Secret?
questions.description: After a suspected leak, security asks for a log of who accessed a specific secret. You find there is no record. What went wrong and what fixes it?
questions.situation: There is a suspicion that a database credential was read by something it should not have been. Security wants the access trail from Vault. You check and realize no audit device was ever enabled, so Vault kept no record of past requests. You need an answer now and a fix so this never happens again.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault audit list
No audit devices are enabled.

$ vault audit enable file file_path=/vault/logs/audit.log
Success! Enabled the file audit device at: file/

# sample entry: request path and identity logged, secret values HMAC'd
options: Enable an audit device now; it records every request and response going forward, with sensitive strings HMAC'd, but it cannot recover past activity
Read the access history from the root token's lookup output, which tracks every secret it ever touched
Check the standard server stdout logs, which always contain the full plaintext of every secret read
Re-enable the secrets engine, which rebuilds and exposes the historical access log for that path
questions.explanation: Audit devices are the access trail, and they only record from the moment you enable them. With none enabled, there is no retroactive history to pull, which is exactly why turning one on should be part of the initial bring-up. Once enabled, every request and response is logged, with sensitive values HMAC'd so the log itself is not a secrets leak; you can still match a known value by HMAC'ing it with the same key. Token lookup shows a token's policies and TTL, not a history of secrets it read. Server stdout is operational logging and does not contain secret values. Re-enabling a secrets engine does not reconstruct any access history and would disrupt the mount.
questions.hint: Vault does not log access unless you tell it to, and it cannot show you anything from before that was turned on. Think about what should have been enabled on day one.

questions.title: Manual Unseal Doesn't Scale
questions.description: Your Vault nodes restart often and each one needs three people to paste unseal keys. How do you make restarts hands-off without weakening security?
questions.situation: Vault runs in an autoscaling group and nodes get recycled during patching and scaling. Every new node comes up sealed, and someone has to gather three of the five key holders to unseal it, sometimes at 3am. You want nodes to unseal themselves on boot while keeping a strong recovery process for emergencies.
options: Write the unseal key shares to a file on each node so a startup script can paste them in automatically
Configure auto-unseal with a cloud KMS (or Transit) seal so nodes unseal on boot; Shamir shares become recovery keys
Lower the unseal threshold to 1 so a single on-call person can unseal each node quickly
Disable the seal entirely in non-public networks so nodes start unsealed and skip the step
questions.explanation: Auto-unseal hands the unsealing step to a trusted external key, usually a cloud KMS or another Vault's Transit engine. On boot a node asks the KMS to decrypt its root key and unseals itself, so autoscaling and patching no longer page anyone. Your Shamir shares are converted into recovery keys, which you keep for break-glass and for operations like generating a new root token. Storing the unseal shares on disk recreates a master key sitting next to the data, which is the risk sealing exists to prevent. Dropping the threshold to 1 destroys the split-knowledge guarantee. There is no option to skip the seal; Vault always boots sealed by design, and trusting the network is not a substitute for protecting the root key.
questions.hint: The goal is no human in the loop on a normal restart, without leaving the keys lying around. What trusted external service can hold the key that unwraps Vault's root key?

questions.title: A Node Got Popped
questions.description: One app server was compromised. Its Vault token had issued many dynamic database and cloud credentials. How do you contain the blast fast?
questions.situation: Security confirms one application host was compromised. While it was up, its Vault token requested a stream of dynamic Postgres and AWS credentials, all still within their leases. You need to invalidate everything that token caused to be issued, immediately, and at the backend so the database users and cloud keys actually stop working.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault list sys/leases/lookup/database/creds/app
Keys
----
3xKp...
9fA2...
# plus AWS creds under aws/creds/app

$ vault lease revoke -prefix aws/creds/app
$ vault token revoke <compromised-token>
options: Seal Vault, since a sealed server cannot serve secrets and that stops the leaked credentials from working
Rotate the root token and the unseal keys, which cascades and invalidates all child tokens and leases
Revoke the compromised token and use vault lease revoke -prefix to cascade-revoke the dynamic secrets it issued
Delete the affected KV entries so the application can no longer read the values it cached
questions.explanation: Dynamic secrets are tracked as leases, and revocation reaches back to the backend: revoking a database lease drops the Postgres user, revoking an AWS lease deletes the IAM credentials. Revoke the compromised token (which revokes its leases and any child tokens) and run vault lease revoke -prefix on the paths it used to cascade-revoke everything issued under them. Sealing Vault stops new requests but does nothing to the credentials already handed out; the leaked database and cloud creds keep working until their leases end. Rotating the root token and unseal keys does not revoke unrelated leases. The compromised creds here are dynamic, not KV values, so deleting KV entries misses the actual exposure.
questions.hint: These are dynamic credentials backed by leases, and leases can be revoked all the way down to the database and cloud provider. What stops Vault serving new requests is not the same as killing what it already issued.

questions.title: Designing Vault for HA
questions.description: A single Vault node is a single point of failure. You want high availability with fewer moving parts than the old Consul setup. What storage backend do you pick?
questions.situation: Your current Vault is one node backed by a file storage path. If it dies, every service loses secrets. The previous team ran Vault on a separate Consul cluster for HA, which was a lot to operate. You want HA with automatic leader election and as little extra infrastructure as possible.
options: Run several Vault nodes that all point at the same file storage path on a shared NFS volume
Put a load balancer in front of two independent Vault nodes that each use their own local file storage
Keep one node but take frequent snapshots, since fast restore is equivalent to high availability
Use Integrated Storage (Raft): a cluster replicates data across nodes with automatic leader election, no separate Consul
questions.explanation: Integrated Storage (Raft) is the standard HA backend now. You run an odd number of nodes (3 or 5), they replicate the data through the Raft consensus protocol, one is the active node and the rest are standbys, and leader election is automatic when the active node fails. It needs no separate Consul cluster, which is exactly the operational simplification you want. The file backend does not support HA at all, and pointing multiple nodes at one file path or one NFS volume leads to corruption, since it has no locking or replication. Two nodes behind a load balancer with separate local storage are two different Vaults with different data, not an HA cluster. Snapshots are for disaster recovery, not availability; a restore still means downtime.
questions.hint: One option removes the extra Consul cluster while still replicating data across nodes with leader election. Watch out for backends that have no locking or replication, like a shared file path.

questions.title: Delivering the SecretID Safely
questions.description: You need to get an AppRole secret_id to a freshly booted instance without it showing up in logs or being silently stolen in transit. What does response wrapping give you?
questions.situation: Your provisioning system generates an AppRole secret_id and passes it to a new instance so the app can log in to Vault. You are worried the secret_id could be captured in CI logs, a process list, or by something on the path before the app uses it. You want delivery to be single-use and to detect if anyone touched it first.
questions.codeExample:
$ vault write -wrap-ttl=120s -f auth/approle/role/app/secret-id
Key                Value
---                -----
wrapping_token     hvs.CAESxxxx...   # this is handed to the instance
ttl                120s

# app: vault unwrap <wrapping_token>  -> real secret_id, once only
options: Response wrapping encrypts the secret_id with the Transit engine so only the app holding the key can read it
Response wrapping stores the secret_id in a single-use, short-TTL token; if it is intercepted the unwrap fails, revealing the leak
Response wrapping makes the secret_id permanent and reusable so the instance can re-read it on every restart
Response wrapping replaces the secret_id with the role_id, which is safe to pass around in the clear
questions.explanation: Response wrapping returns a single-use wrapping token instead of the secret itself. The real secret_id is held in the wrapping token's private cubbyhole with a short TTL, and only the holder can unwrap it, exactly once. That gives you tamper evidence: if an attacker intercepts the wrapping token and unwraps it, your app's later unwrap fails, so you immediately know the value leaked and can rotate it. This addresses the secret-zero problem of getting the first credential to a workload safely. It is not Transit encryption; the secret lives in a cubbyhole, not encrypted ciphertext you hand around. It does not make the secret permanent (the opposite, it is single-use and short-lived), and it does not swap in the role_id.
questions.hint: The value travels as a one-time token, not the secret itself. Think about what happens to your app's unwrap attempt if someone already used the token, and what that tells you.
